content:
# Joshua Chapter 8 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 231-232 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_008_page_001.jpg`

---

*[The Fall of Ai and the Covenant at Ebal — Thirty-five verses that pivot from shame to triumph and from battlefield to altar. After the humiliation at Ai in chapter 7 and the purging of Achan's sin, YHWH restores Joshua with the same words he used at the Jordan: "Do not be afraid." The military strategy is cunning — an ambush that exploits the overconfidence Ai gained from its first victory. But the chapter does not end with conquest. It ends with Torah. Joshua builds an altar on Mount Ebal, copies the law of Moses onto stones, and reads every word of it to the entire assembly — men, women, children, and foreigners alike. War gives way to worship. The sword gives way to the scroll.]*

---

**1** Then YHWH said to Joshua, "Do not be afraid, and do not lose heart. Take all the fighting men with you and march against Ai. Look — I am handing the king of Ai over to you, along with his people, his city, and his land. **2** Do to Ai and its king what you did to Jericho and its king — except this time you may keep the plunder and the livestock for yourselves. Set an ambush behind the city."

**3** So Joshua and all the fighting men prepared to march against Ai. Joshua chose thirty thousand of his best warriors and sent them out at night **4** with these orders: "Listen carefully — you are to set an ambush behind the city. Do not go very far from it. All of you, stay ready. **5** I and all the troops with me will advance on the city. When they come out to meet us, just as they did before, we will run from them. **6** They will chase us until we have drawn them away from the city, because they will think, 'They are running from us just like last time.' So we will flee from them. **7** Then you are to rise up from the ambush and seize the city. YHWH your God will hand it over to you. **8** Once you have taken the city, set it on fire. Do as YHWH has commanded. Those are your orders."

**9** Joshua sent them off, and they went to the place of ambush and took up a position between Bethel and Ai, to the west of Ai. Joshua spent that night among the people.

**10** Early the next morning Joshua mustered the troops, and he and the elders of Israel marched at the head of the army toward Ai. **11** All the fighting men with him advanced until they came near the city and set up camp on the north side. There was a valley between them and Ai. **12** He had taken about five thousand men and positioned them as an ambush between Bethel and Ai, to the west of the city. **13** So the army was in position — the main camp to the north of the city and the rear guard to its west. That night Joshua went down into the valley.

**14** When the king of Ai saw this, he and all the men of the city rushed out early in the morning to engage Israel in battle. He went to the designated place facing the Arabah — not knowing that an ambush had been set behind the city. **15** Joshua and all Israel let themselves be beaten back and fled toward the wilderness. **16** Every man in Ai was called out to chase them, and as they pursued Joshua they were drawn away from the city. **17** Not a man was left in Ai or Bethel who had not gone out after Israel. They left the city wide open and chased Israel.

**18** Then YHWH said to Joshua, "Stretch out the javelin in your hand toward Ai, for I am giving it to you." Joshua stretched out the javelin in his hand toward the city. **19** The moment he stretched out his hand, the men in ambush rose quickly from their position, rushed forward, entered the city, captured it, and immediately set it on fire.

**20** When the men of Ai looked back, they saw smoke rising from their city into the sky — and they had no way to flee in any direction. The Israelites who had been fleeing toward the wilderness now turned on their pursuers, **21** for when Joshua and all Israel saw that the ambush had taken the city and that smoke was going up from it, they turned around and struck down the men of Ai. **22** The others came out of the city against them from the other side, so the men of Ai were caught in the middle, with Israelites on both sides. Israel struck them down until no survivor or fugitive remained. **23** But the king of Ai they captured alive and brought him to Joshua.

**24** When Israel had finished killing all the inhabitants of Ai in the open field — in the wilderness where they had chased them — and every last one of them had fallen by the sword, all Israel returned to Ai and put it to the sword. **25** The total number who fell that day, both men and women, was twelve thousand — all the people of Ai. **26** For Joshua did not draw back the hand that held the outstretched javelin until he had devoted to destruction all the inhabitants of Ai. **27** Only the livestock and the plunder of the city did Israel keep for themselves, according to the word that YHWH had spoken to Joshua.

**28** So Joshua burned Ai and made it a permanent ruin, a desolation to this day. **29** He hung the king of Ai on a tree and left him there until evening. At sunset, Joshua gave the order, and they took the body down from the tree and threw it at the entrance of the city gate. They raised a great pile of stones over it, which remains to this day.

**30** Then Joshua built an altar to YHWH, the God of Israel, on Mount Ebal, **31** just as Moses the servant of YHWH had commanded the Sons of Israel — as it is written in the book of the law of Moses: an altar of uncut stones on which no iron tool had been used. On it they offered burnt offerings to YHWH and sacrificed peace offerings. **32** And there, on the stones, Joshua wrote a copy of the law of Moses, which he had written, in the presence of the Sons of Israel.

**33** All Israel — their elders, officers, and judges — stood on both sides of the ark, facing the Levitical priests who carried the ark of the covenant of YHWH. Foreigners and native-born alike were there. Half of them stood in front of Mount Gerizim and half in front of Mount Ebal, just as Moses the servant of YHWH had commanded earlier for the blessing of the people of Israel.

**34** After this, Joshua read aloud every word of the law — the blessings and the curses — exactly as they are written in the book of the law. **35** There was not a single word of all that Moses had commanded that Joshua did not read aloud before the entire assembly of Israel — including the women, the children, and the foreigners living among them.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 1 — "Do not be afraid, and do not lose heart" (al-tira v'al-tekhath):** The same pair of commands YHWH gave at the beginning of the conquest (1:9). After the disaster at Ai and the sin of Achan, YHWH reissues the charge. The verb khathath means to be shattered, to be dismayed — it is the fear that paralyzes. PNEUMA renders it "lose heart" to distinguish it from raw terror (yare'). The repetition signals a reset: the failed campaign is behind; YHWH is re-commissioning Joshua as if beginning again.

**V. 2 — "Except this time you may keep the plunder":** The Hebrew raq shlalah u-vhemtah tavozu lakhem — "only its spoil and its livestock you may take for yourselves." This is the cruel irony of Achan's sin. Had he waited one battle, looting would have been permitted. At Jericho, everything was kherem (devoted to YHWH). At Ai, the plunder belongs to the soldiers. PNEUMA makes this contrast clear with "except this time."

**V. 3 — Thirty thousand vs. five thousand (vv. 3, 12):** The MT reads thirty thousand (sheloshim eleph) in v. 3 and five thousand (khamesheth alaphim) in v. 12. Some scholars view the numbers as contradictory (two sources merged), but the text can be read as two separate units: the thirty thousand as the total ambush force sent at night, and the five thousand as a secondary detachment positioned closer to the city. The LXX Vaticanus has a significantly shorter text for this chapter, omitting several verses. PNEUMA follows the MT but notes the tension.

**V. 9 — "Joshua spent that night among the people":** The Hebrew vayyalen ha-laylah ha-hu b'tokhh ha-'am — he lodged that night in the midst of the people. After the private failure of chapter 7, Joshua is now embedded with his troops. The detail is brief but poignant: the commander sleeps among his soldiers on the eve of battle.

**V. 13 — "Joshua went down into the valley" (ha-'emeq):** The Hebrew 'emeq is a broad valley or lowland between ridges. Joshua positions himself in the exposed low ground between the main force and Ai — making himself visible bait. The king of Ai would see the Israelite camp across the valley and be drawn out. PNEUMA keeps "valley" and lets the tactical picture speak for itself.

**V. 18 — "Stretch out the javelin" (kidon):** The word kidon refers to a short javelin or scimitar-like weapon — not the same as the staff (matteh) Moses raised. Yet the gesture consciously echoes Moses raising his hands over Amalek (Exodus 17:8-13). As long as Joshua holds the weapon outstretched, Israel prevails (v. 26). YHWH gives the tactical command directly: this is not Joshua's improvisation but divine choreography. PNEUMA translates kidon as "javelin" (its most common identification) and preserves the dramatic simultaneity of the raised hand and the ambush springing.

**V. 26 — "Joshua did not draw back the hand":** The Hebrew lo heshiv yado — "he did not bring back his hand." This is the passage's most striking image. Joshua stands with arm extended, javelin pointed toward the burning city, and holds that posture until every inhabitant is destroyed. It is Moses at the Red Sea. It is Moses over Amalek. The outstretched hand of the leader channels the power of YHWH. PNEUMA makes the connection vivid without importing the Exodus parallels explicitly — letting the reader feel the echo.

**V. 28 — "A permanent ruin, a desolation" (tel-'olam shemamah):** The Hebrew tel is a mound — specifically the kind of artificial hill created by layers of destroyed cities. The word entered English as "tell" (as in archaeological "Tell es-Sultan"). 'Olam means perpetual, everlasting. Shemamah means wasteland, horror. PNEUMA renders "a permanent ruin, a desolation" — the city will never be rebuilt. Archaeology identifies et-Tell or Khirbet el-Maqatir as possible Ai sites; both show destruction layers consistent with this description.

**V. 29 — "Hung the king on a tree":** The Hebrew talah 'al ha-'etz — to hang or impale on a wooden pole. This is post-mortem display, not execution by hanging. Deuteronomy 21:22-23 mandates that a body hung on a tree must be taken down before nightfall, "for a hanged man is cursed by God." Joshua obeys this law precisely — the body comes down at sunset. PNEUMA renders this straightforwardly and notes that the stone cairn over the corpse at the gate is both a memorial and a marker of shame, paralleling the cairn over Achan in 7:26.

**V. 30-35 — The altar at Ebal:** The chapter's final six verses shift abruptly from warfare to worship, fulfilling Moses' command in Deuteronomy 27:1-8. The altar is built of uncut stones (avenim shelemot — "whole stones," untouched by metal tools, per Exodus 20:25). Joshua writes a "copy of the law of Moses" (mishneh torath mosheh) on the stones — not the entire Torah, but likely a summary or the covenant terms of Deuteronomy. The LXX Vaticanus places this Ebal ceremony after 9:2 rather than here. PNEUMA follows the MT placement. The theological logic is deliberate: before any further conquest, Israel renews its covenant identity. The sword must answer to the scroll.

**V. 33 — "Foreigners and native-born alike" (ka-ger ka-'ezrakh):** The ger (sojourner, resident alien) stands alongside the ezrakh (native citizen) in this covenant assembly. The Torah is not read only to ethnic Israel. Everyone within the community — including the foreigners who have joined themselves to Israel — hears the blessings and the curses. PNEUMA preserves "foreigners" rather than the traditional "sojourner" for clarity, and "native-born" rather than "citizen" to avoid modern political connotations.

**V. 35 — "Not a single word":** The Hebrew lo hayah davar mikkol asher tzivvah mosheh asher lo qara yehoshua — literally, "there was not a word from all that Moses commanded that Joshua did not read." The double negative is emphatic: absolutely nothing was omitted. Every blessing, every curse, every stipulation. And the audience is total: women, children, foreigners. The chapter ends not with the smoke of Ai but with the sound of Torah. PNEUMA gives this verse its full weight as the theological climax of the chapter.
